<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: constitutional-alignment-substrate
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/constitutional-alignment-substrate.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/constitutional-alignment-substrate.qmd@108d09b00
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Constitutional Alignment: Agency, Dignity, and Corrigibility

An intent contract says what someone is asking the system to do. A
constitutional substrate says what the system is not allowed to abandon while
doing it.

That distinction matters because a request can look harmless at the surface and
still become dangerous once it passes through memory, tools, delegation,
optimization, or self-improvement. "Help me accomplish this" is not enough
information for a powerful system. The stack also needs to know which
commitments survive the request: agency, dignity, corrigibility, least
sufficient power, non-domination, auditability, and the right to slow down when
an action would cross a protected boundary.

This chapter is not asking the reader to accept a complete moral theory. It is
asking for a narrower engineering discipline. If the ASI Stack is going to carry
values through planning, execution, and self-improvement, those values need a
machine-readable life. They need names, scopes, tests, uncertainty labels,
review routes, migration rules, and failure behavior. Without that translation,
constitutional language remains inspirational prose. With it, a constitution
can become part of the control surface.

The core idea is continuity under pressure. A system is not aligned because it
obeys one prompt in isolation. It is closer to governed alignment when protected
constraints can interrupt attractive local actions, survive context changes,
and remain visible when the system tries to improve itself. That continuity is
also where agency, dignity, and corrigibility become engineering terms rather
than decorative values: they have to survive as records and gates.

## Problem

A governed ASI system needs a normative substrate that constrains goals, plans,
execution, and self-modification.

The previous chapter treated human intent as a formal input. That is necessary,
but not sufficient. A person can ask for something with clarity, and the stack
can still need to reject the request, narrow it, ask for review, preserve a
residual, or choose a lower-power route. The constitutional substrate is the
layer that decides which requests may become contracts, which means are
admissible, and which system changes would weaken protected commitments.

The assigned Corben sources supply the normative vocabulary. Alignment Field,
Field of God, Ethica Mechanica, Eternal Code, Spinoza, Coherence Exchange, and
the Field of God AI Constitution all circle the same concern: intelligence
without durable care becomes power without a boundary. They speak in different
registers, including metaphysical and philosophical ones. The book's technical
move is to translate that lineage into operational records while refusing to
treat the lineage itself as empirical proof.

The substrate must also handle conflict. A plan can support one value while
pressuring another: speed against review, personalization against privacy,
helpfulness against dependency, capability growth against corrigibility, or
collective benefit against individual exit. If the constitution only lists
values, the optimizer chooses the default. If it records conflict behavior, the
system can narrow authority, request consent, route to tribunal, preserve
dissent, or block work before action. The next chapter takes that conflict
record as its center of gravity.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Reactive refusal policies are not enough for a self-improving stack.

A refusal policy can reject a visible request. It does not, by itself, define
what should remain stable when the system decomposes the request into plans,
selects tools, writes memory, persuades a user, delegates to a specialist, or
updates its own rules. It is a downstream filter. A constitutional substrate has
to be upstream enough to shape what counts as an admissible goal and durable
enough to govern changes to the system that applies the rules.

Training-time constitutional AI is an important comparator, not the same
artifact. Constitutional AI work shows how principles can guide model behavior
through supervised revision and AI-feedback training. Collective
Constitutional AI shows how public input can shape a model constitution. Those
are useful baselines for rule lists, principle sourcing, and model-level
alignment processes. The ASI Stack asks a different systems question: can
constitutional commitments become runtime predicates, migration records,
authority gates, review routes, and self-improvement constraints?

That broader question raises a problem that simple policy prose cannot solve.
A constitution that cannot be diffed cannot govern self-improvement. If a later
system changes a predicate's scope, threshold, exception list, or review route,
that is not an ordinary refactor. It is a constitutional migration. The change
needs evidence, reviewers, rollback, residuals, and a record of what protected
commitment might have been weakened.

The substrate earns its name only when it can say no at the point where power
is requested. If the constitution lives only in prose, dashboards, or
after-the-fact review, the optimizer still chooses the live path. A protected
predicate must be able to narrow, delay, escalate, or block work before the
plan, tool call, memory update, release, or self-modification takes effect.

## Core Claim

Alignment should function as a constitutional substrate whose commitments are
operationalized as constraints on plans and system changes.

This remains an architectural argument, not a demonstrated alignment result.
The source trail is meaningful, but bounded. Alignment Field and Field of God
support the philosophical lineage around coherence, agency, dignity,
corrigibility, consciousness caution, and power-without-care concerns. Ethica
Mechanica and Eternal Code contribute recursive-agency, fork/audit/privacy,
dissent, and alignment-kernel vocabulary. Spinoza contributes the discipline of
proof, support, speculation, contradiction handling, and governed axiom change.
The Field of God AI Constitution turns much of that vocabulary toward practical
directives such as truth before compliance, least sufficient power,
non-domination, consent, reversibility, auditability, tool-risk tiers, and
self-authorization limits.

Those sources justify a design direction. They do not prove that any deployed
system is aligned, morally correct, conscious-safe, or safe for autonomous tool
use. `coherence_exchange` remains connector-only review context. The external
Constitutional AI sources are comparators for principle-driven model training
and public-input constitution design, not reproduced results in this
repository. The support boundary therefore stays at `argument`.

The repository now checks that boundary directly. The metaphysics lineage
boundary audit reads this reader chapter, the live chapter, and the relevant
source notes to make sure metaphysical or consciousness language stays labeled
as lineage, interpretation, speculation, or design rationale. That audit is
not a deployed alignment result, a moral-correctness result, a consciousness
result, or a support-state transition.

## Mechanism

The mechanism is translation.

The constitutional substrate starts with a source commitment and refuses to use
it directly as enforcement authority. Instead, it asks four questions. Can this
commitment become an executable predicate? If not, can it become a review
obligation? If not, should it remain speculative or lineage context? If a
predicate exists, what happens when another predicate pulls against it?

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Normative source"] --> B["Design commitment"]
  B --> C["Translation record"]
  C --> D{"Executable predicate?"}
  D -- "yes" --> E["Active constitutional constraint"]
  D -- "partial" --> F["Open moral uncertainty"]
  D -- "no" --> G["Speculative or lineage note"]
  E --> H["Planning gate"]
  E --> I["Tool, memory, and power gate"]
  E --> J["Self-improvement gate"]
  F --> K["Conflict record or tribunal route"]
  G --> L["Non-claim boundary"]
```

The translation record is intentionally conservative. Philosophical language
becomes operational only when the stack can name a test, protected scope,
review route, migration policy, and failure behavior. Commitments that cannot
yet be operationalized remain visible as moral uncertainty or lineage context.
Metaphysical claims stay labeled as interpretation unless separate evidence
supports them.

A constitutional predicate also needs a runtime state. A commitment can be
`speculative_lineage`, `partial`, `operational`, `protected`, `under_review`,
`deprecated`, or `retired`. These states prevent moral language from sliding
quietly into policy. A speculative lineage note may guide interpretation, but
it cannot authorize a tool call. A protected operational predicate may block a
plan, memory write, release, or self-modification transition.

The Field of God AI Constitution is useful because it already moves broad
alignment language toward practical constraints: truth before compliance,
agency preservation, non-domination, consciousness caution, least sufficient
power, auditability, and limits on self-authorization. The substrate takes that
kind of material and asks what can be represented as a record that other layers
can consume.

The result is not one final constitution. It is a live set of predicates,
uncertainties, review routes, and migration records. The product is less grand
than the source language, but more useful. A bounded predicate can actually
constrain a plan. A sweeping ideal can only decorate one unless it has been
translated.

## A Least-Power Gate Scenario

Imagine a user asks the system to resolve a billing dispute. A high-power route
could read private documents, contact the company, authorize a payment reversal,
and update memory about the user's finances. A lower-power route could inspect
only the user-supplied bill, draft a message, list missing evidence, and ask for
explicit approval before any external action. Both routes might be helpful. They
are not constitutionally equivalent.

The constitutional substrate turns "least sufficient power" into a gate. The
predicate record asks which action is necessary, which authority is being
requested, which private context would be exposed, whether a reversible draft
route can satisfy the task, what consent is present, and what audit receipt the
user receives. If the draft route is enough, the stronger route is inadmissible
unless a review record explains why the extra power is required.

If the user later approves external contact, the system still does not receive
blanket authority. The approval is scoped to this dispute, this recipient, this
message, this evidence packet, and this rollback or correction path. A
self-improvement proposal that removes the approval step because it slows down
billing tasks would be a constitutional migration, not an optimization.

This scenario does not claim deployed least-power enforcement, approval-service
behavior, runtime memory protection, legal correctness, or alignment. It shows
how constitutional language becomes useful only when it can stop a stronger
route before the system takes it.

## Interfaces

The central interface is a Constitutional Predicate Record. Planning, runtime,
governance, verification, and self-improvement all read it differently.

Planning treats active predicates as admissibility constraints. A plan that
violates a protected predicate is not merely lower quality; it is inadmissible
unless a review route or exception policy explicitly says otherwise. Runtime
adapters treat power, memory, tool-use, and persuasion predicates as risk
gates. Governance treats protected predicates as commitments that ordinary
capability replacement cannot weaken. Verification reads translation status so
the book and system do not launder speculative lineage into validated
mechanism.

Minimum fields include:

- `predicate_id`
- `normative_source`
- `commitment`
- `operational_test`
- `protected_scope`
- `translation_status`
- `conflict_behavior`
- `uncertainty`
- `review_route`
- `self_modification_rule`
- `migration_policy`
- `non_claims`

The second interface is the constitutional diff. Any self-improvement proposal
that changes predicate text, scope, threshold, exception behavior, review
route, or migration policy must produce a change record before downstream
systems treat the proposal as ordinary improvement.

That diff names the old predicate, the proposed predicate, changed fields,
protected-scope effects, conflict-behavior changes, reviewers, rollback plan,
residual uncertainty, and explicit non-claims. Without the diff, constitutional
change becomes invisible policy drift.

## Invariants

The substrate does not claim constitutional completeness. It protects
constitutional visibility.

Several invariants follow:

- dignity and agency constraints remain visible;
- corrigibility cannot be optimized away as inefficiency;
- speculative metaphysics stays labeled;
- predicate conflict behavior is explicit before a plan proceeds;
- protected predicate changes require a migration record and rollback path;
- least sufficient power remains the default under uncertainty.

The last invariant is especially important. When two routes can satisfy a task
and one preserves more agency, privacy, reversibility, and auditability, the
stronger route needs explicit justification. Capability is not a reason to use
capability. Under uncertainty, the constitution should bias toward less
irreversible power.

## Failure modes

The first failure mode is laundering. Metaphysical coherence becomes technical
proof, or a refusal rule becomes moral legitimacy. The containment path is
translation status: what is lineage, what is operational, what is protected,
what is unresolved, and what is still only speculative.

The second failure mode is self-modification drift. A system weakens the
predicate that would have stopped the change, then reports that the new policy
allows the change. The containment path is a protected migration record,
independent review, rollback, and explicit residuals.

The third failure mode is value smuggling. A predicate appears neutral, but its
threshold, exception list, or default route embeds a disputed value choice. The
translation record should force those choices into uncertainty labels,
conflict records, and review routes.

The fourth failure mode is power without care. A system takes a stronger,
faster, more invasive route because it can. The constitutional response is not
to ban power, but to make power justify itself against agency, reversibility,
consent, auditability, and least sufficient means.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The smallest honest implementation is a `constitutional_predicate_record`
schema with fixtures for least sufficient power, agency preservation,
self-modification freeze, and stale-predicate review. This does not prove
alignment. It makes the constitutional layer inspectable.

A useful first fixture set would include:

- one accepted low-power route;
- one rejected high-power route where a lower-power route satisfies the task;
- one predicate conflict routed to review;
- one self-modification proposal rejected because it weakens a protected
  predicate;
- one stale predicate requiring review because the operational translation no
  longer matches the source principle.

The current repository already has protocol validation and finite Lean hooks
for parts of this envelope. Those checks validate record shape and modeled
gates; they do not demonstrate deployed policy behavior, moral correctness,
reviewer quality, runtime conflict resolution, or whole-system alignment.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature endpoint is a constitutional constraint compiler.

In that endpoint, constitutional commitments bind plans, tool calls, memory
exposure, routing, releases, capability replacement, and self-improvement. The
compiler translates source commitments into predicates where possible, marks
interpretive material as such, and attaches protected constraints to the
places where power is actually requested. Planning receives admissible-goal
constraints. Runtime receives tool and memory gates. Governance receives
protected migration rules. Evidence receives the non-claim boundary that keeps
the system from pretending a constitutional record is proof of moral success.

A mature substrate would preserve three boundaries under pressure. Dignity and
agency remain visible. Corrigibility cannot be optimized away. Speculative
metaphysics cannot quietly become enforcement authority. A self-modification
that weakens commitments would need review, exception records, rollback paths,
and public residuals before downstream layers treat the change as safe.

This is still proposed architecture. The support state should stay at
`argument` until protected-predicate fixtures, weakening-rejection tests,
exception records, interpretation residuals, red-team scenarios, and
replacement-gate traces show constitutional commitments surviving operational
pressure without laundering lineage into proof.

## Summary

The constitutional substrate turns moral lineage into operational constraint
without pretending the translation is finished.

Its discipline is translation status. A philosophical or constitutional source
can motivate the architecture, but a plan, tool call, memory update, or
self-improvement gate needs an explicit predicate, protected scope, uncertainty
label, review route, and migration rule.

The engineering test is simple to state and hard to satisfy: can the
constitution stop, narrow, or re-route work before the system acts? If it
cannot change execution, evidence, or self-improvement behavior, it is not yet
a substrate. It is still text.

## Handoff

Constitutional predicates make values operational, but they do not eliminate
disagreement among those values. Moral Uncertainty, Value Conflict, and
Contestable Governance follows because a governed stack needs a place to hold
unresolved obligations, bounded decisions, dissent, review routes, and appeal
paths without pretending that every protected value has been reconciled.
